summary: Italian verismo opera composer, best known for Andrea Chénier (1896), which depicts the French Revolutionary poet of the same name executed during the Reign of Terror alongside his lover Maddalena. Also composed Fedora and Madame Sans-Gêne. Associated with the verismo movement alongside Mascagni and Leoncavallo.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Umberto Menotti Maria Giordano (1867–1948) was an Italian opera composer and central figure of the verismo movement, alongside Pietro Mascagni and Ruggero Leoncavallo. His most famous work is Andrea Chénier (1896). He is also known for Fedora (1898) and Madame Sans-Gêne (1915). The tenor Enrico Caruso launched to fame with his performance in Fedora. Wikipedia

examples: Enrico Caruso shot to fame after his performance as Loris, who sings 'Amor ti vieta' in this composer's opera Fedora.

works.name: Andrea Chénier
works.description: Giordano's most famous opera (1896), set during the French Revolution and Reign of Terror. The title character is a real-life French poet who is executed by guillotine along with his lover Maddalena de Coigny. The opera features the aria 'La mamma morta,' sung by Maddalena to the baritone Gerard, and 'L'Improvviso' ('Un dì all'azzurro spazio'), an aria by the title character. Libretto by Luigi Illica. Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'La mamma morta' — soprano aria sung by Maddalena to Gerard; she recalls Love telling her to keep living after her house was burned and her mother was killed; she offers her body to Gerard to save André
examples: In this composer's best-known aria, the singer remembers Love telling her to keep on living after her house was burned and her mother killed.
Maddalena agrees to give herself to Gerard in order to save the title character in this opera's most famous aria, 'La mamma morta.'

clues.clue: Ends with the title poet and Maddalena both executed by guillotine during the Reign of Terror
examples: name this opera that ends with the deaths by guillotine of Maddalena and her title lover, a French revolutionary poet, written by Umberto Giordano
one that ends with Maddalena going to the guillotine with her lover, the poet Andrea Chenier

clues.clue: Title character's aria 'Un dì all'azzurro spazio' (also called 'L'Improvviso') describes the beauty of nature vs. poor treatment of the lower classes; nicknamed 'Improvviso' because true poetry cannot be made on command
examples: another aria from that opera is sometimes called the 'Improvviso' instead of its first line 'Un di all'azzurro spazio,' because it follows the claim that true poetry cannot be produced on command
This opera's title character describes the beauty of nature in contrast to the poor treatment of the lower classes in an aria also known as 'L'Improvviso,' 'Un di all'azzuro spazio.'

In a later scene from this opera, a character regrets making false accusations against the title character, but the Tribunal still imprisons him and orders him executed.

examples: In a Cyrano-esque scene in this opera, a woman admits to being the one who sent anonymous love letters to the title character; those two characters then pledge their love for each other in 'Ora soave.'

clues.clue: Was once staged on a floating stage on Lake Constance
examples: In this Verismo opera by Umberto Giordano, Madeleine offers her body to the evil Gerard in an attempt to save her lover. It was once staged on a floating stage on Lake Constance.

clues.clue: 'La mamma morta' contains the lines 'A dying body is my body / So take it. / I am already dead matter'; Maria Callas's version was used in the film Philadelphia
examples: Madeleine offers her body in this soprano aria that includes the lines 'A dying body is my body / So take it. / I am already dead matter.' The Maria Callas version of it was used in the film Philadelphia.

works.name: Fedora

comprehensive_summary:
Umberto Menotti Maria Giordano (1867–1948) was an Italian opera composer and one of the principal figures of the verismo movement in opera, alongside Pietro Mascagni (Cavalleria Rusticana) and Ruggero Leoncavallo (I Pagliacci). His operas focus on realistic, emotionally intense subjects and were staples of the late 19th- and early 20th-century Italian operatic repertoire.

Giordano's most celebrated work is Andrea Chénier (1896), with a libretto by Luigi Illica (who also wrote Puccini's La Bohème, Tosca, and Madama Butterfly in collaboration with Giacosa). The opera is set during the French Revolution and Reign of Terror. The title character is a real historical French poet who is eventually executed by guillotine alongside his lover Maddalena de Coigny. The opera opens with a scene in which a servant's son (Gerard) watches his elderly father struggle to move furniture and adjusts cushions on a blue sofa — described as an apostrophe to the sofa. In Act II, the spy l'Incrédibile sees through the maid Bersi's disguise at the Café Hottot while following the title poet. A Cyrano-esque scene has Maddalena admit to sending anonymous love letters to the title character; they pledge their love in 'Ora soave.' The baritone Gerard, a jealous revolutionary, makes accusations against the title character but eventually retracts them; the Revolutionary Tribunal nonetheless sentences Andrea to death. The most famous aria is 'La mamma morta,' a soprano aria sung by Maddalena to Gerard, in which she recalls how Love encouraged her to keep living after her home was burned down and her mother killed — she then offers her body ('A dying body is my body / So take it. / I am already dead matter') to save her poet lover. The Maria Callas recording of this aria was featured in the 1993 film Philadelphia. The title character's aria 'Un dì all'azzurro spazio' (nicknamed 'L'Improvviso') has him describe the beauty of nature against the suffering of the poor; it is called the 'Improvviso' because it follows an argument that true poetry cannot be produced on command. Gerard's baritone aria 'Nemico della Patria' has him question his own motives. Notably, celebrity sopranos (Elena Obraztsova, Rosalind Plowright, Fedora Barbieri) have made cameo appearances in productions playing the small role of old blind Madelon.

Giordano's second most-clued opera is Fedora (1898), based on a play by Victorien Sardou (who refused Giordano twice before granting permission). The opera features the Russian princess Fedora accusing the nihilist Loris Ipanov of murdering her fiancé Vladimir. Enrico Caruso achieved fame through his performance in the premiere as Loris, who sings the aria 'Amor ti vieta.' Giordano also composed Madame Sans-Gêne (1915), in which the title character reminds Napoleon Bonaparte that he owes her sixty francs for laundry.
